title: Malaysian Char Kway Teow

ingredients.name: flat rice noodles (kway teow), fresh or dried
visual_checks: if dried, soak in warm water 15 min; fresh noodles are best
ingredients.notes: if dried, soak in warm water 15 min; fresh noodles are best

subs.name: any flat noodle — wide rice noodles or even fresh pasta noodles work
subs.notes: fresh noodles have better texture than dried

subs.name: 150 g chicken slices or tofu for a non-seafood version
subs.notes: cook chicken 4 minutes before adding noodles

subs.name: shredded cabbage
subs.notes: add 1 minute earlier to allow it to soften slightly

ingredients.name: spring onions, cut in 3 cm pieces
dark soy sauce
visual_checks: dark soy gives the characteristic colour; use 1.5 tbsp dark + 0.5 tbsp light if you have both
ingredients.notes: dark soy gives the characteristic colour; use 1.5 tbsp dark + 0.5 tbsp light if you have both

subs.name: 0.5 tsp extra soy sauce
subs.notes: loses some depth but works fine

steps.text: If using dried noodles, soak in warm water for 15 minutes until pliable, then drain well. Mix soy sauce, fish sauce and chilli sauce in a small bowl.
Heat a wok or kadai on the highest flame for 2 minutes until smoking. This is the single most important step — the heat creates wok hei (the smoky breath of the wok). Add oil and let it shimmer.
Add garlic and green chilli and stir for 15 seconds. Add prawn and stir-fry for 1.5–2 minutes until just pink — remove and set aside.
Add the drained noodles and press them against the wok in a single layer. Leave undisturbed for 30 seconds to char slightly, then toss. Repeat once more.
Pour the sauce mixture over the noodles and toss on high heat for 1 minute until every strand is dark and coated.
Push noodles aside. Crack both eggs into the space and scramble quickly, then fold through the noodles before they fully set.

Return the prawn. Add bean sprouts and spring onion, toss for 30 seconds — the sprouts should still have a crunch. Taste for salt. Dish up and eat immediately; the noodles lose their texture if they sit.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: prawn → 150 g chicken slices or tofu for a non-seafood version (cook chicken 4 minutes before adding noodles); noodles → any flat noodle — wide rice noodles or even fresh pasta noodles work (fresh noodles have better texture than dried); fish-sauce → 0.5 tsp extra soy sauce (loses some depth but works fine); sprouts → shredded cabbage (add 1 minute earlier to allow it to soften slightly)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A kadai on the highest flame is the best Indian substitute for a wok. Let it heat until almost smoking before adding oil. Rice noodles are available at South Indian stores as wide kozhukattai noodles or flat rice pasta from specialty stores. Dark soy sauce is sold in supermarkets. Fish sauce is available online and in gourmet grocery stores; soy sauce with a pinch of salt is a workable substitute.
